610: 	if(n1 != 3 && n2 != 3 && n3 != 3){


611: 		#pragma omp for


612: 		for(i3 = 0; i3 < mm3-1; i3++){


613: 			for(i2 = 0; i2 < mm2-1; i2++){


614: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1; i1++){


615: 					z1[i1] = z[i3][i2+1][i1] + z[i3][i2][i1];


616: 					z2[i1] = z[i3+1][i2][i1] + z[i3][i2][i1];


617: 					z3[i1] = z[i3+1][i2+1][i1] + z[i3+1][i2][i1] + z1[i1];


618: 				}


619: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1-1; i1++){


620: 					u[2*i3][2*i2][2*i1] = u[2*i3][2*i2][2*i1]


621: 						+z[i3][i2][i1];


622: 					u[2*i3][2*i2][2*i1+1] = u[2*i3][2*i2][2*i1+1]


623: 						+0.5*(z[i3][i2][i1+1]+z[i3][i2][i1]);


624: 				}


625: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1-1; i1++){


626: 					u[2*i3][2*i2+1][2*i1] = u[2*i3][2*i2+1][2*i1]


627: 						+0.5 * z1[i1];


628: 					u[2*i3][2*i2+1][2*i1+1] = u[2*i3][2*i2+1][2*i1+1]


629: 						+0.25*( z1[i1] + z1[i1+1] );


630: 				}


631: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1-1; i1++){


632: 					u[2*i3+1][2*i2][2*i1] = u[2*i3+1][2*i2][2*i1]


633: 						+0.5 * z2[i1];


634: 					u[2*i3+1][2*i2][2*i1+1] = u[2*i3+1][2*i2][2*i1+1]


635: 						+0.25*( z2[i1] + z2[i1+1] );


636: 				}


637: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1-1; i1++){


638: 					u[2*i3+1][2*i2+1][2*i1] = u[2*i3+1][2*i2+1][2*i1]


639: 						+0.25* z3[i1];


640: 					u[2*i3+1][2*i2+1][2*i1+1] = u[2*i3+1][2*i2+1][2*i1+1]


641: 						+0.125*( z3[i1] + z3[i1+1] );

Functioninterp(void*, int, int, int, void*, int, int, int, int)

          
Source file and linesmg.cpp:610-1228

        
Modulemg_omp_cpp_g++

        

The loop is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:610-641,971,1219,1228.

Analyzed code is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:610,613-616,620,625-626,631-635,1219,1228.

Warnings:
  • Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
  • Ignoring paths for analysis
  • Failed to get the number of paths (too many, greater than internal limit = 100000)
  • RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
  • Streams not analyzed since number of paths is very high. Force analysis with --force-loop-streams-analysis: expect potentially high RAM/CPU footprint

Try to simplify control.

      

  

    
    
      
  • gain
    •

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.17 to 15.00 cycles (1.94x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 53% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.17 to 23.52 cycles (1.24x speedup).

Details


47% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 59% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 37% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 33% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


FMA


Detected 48 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.


  


Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • ADD: 1 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:625

Conversion instructions


Detected expensive conversion instructions.

Details


  • CLTQ: 2 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:620
    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:625


Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Type of elements and instruction set


9 AVX-512 instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (eight at a time).


Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop


The binary loop is composed of 120 FP arithmetical operations:

  • 72: addition or subtraction (48 inside FMA instructions)
  • 48: multiply (all inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 1156 bytes (144 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 444 bytes (55 double precision FP elements).

Arithmetic intensity


Arithmetic intensity is 0.07 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 29.17 cycles. At this rate:

  • 41% of peak load performance is reached (39.63 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 23% of peak store performance is reached (15.22 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.17 to 22.33 cycles (1.31x speedup).

  • Ignoring paths for analysis

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.17 to 15.00 cycles (1.94x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 53% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.17 to 23.52 cycles (1.24x speedup).

Details


47% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 59% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 37% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 33% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


FMA


Detected 48 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.


  


Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • ADD: 1 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:625
  • VMOVUPD: 6 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:632

Conversion instructions


Detected expensive conversion instructions.

Details


  • CLTQ: 2 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:620
    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:625


Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Type of elements and instruction set


9 AVX-512 instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (eight at a time).


Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop


The binary loop is composed of 120 FP arithmetical operations:

  • 72: addition or subtraction (48 inside FMA instructions)
  • 48: multiply (all inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 1156 bytes (144 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 444 bytes (55 double precision FP elements).

Arithmetic intensity


Arithmetic intensity is 0.07 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 29.17 cycles. At this rate:

  • 41% of peak load performance is reached (39.63 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 23% of peak store performance is reached (15.22 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.17 to 22.33 cycles (1.31x speedup).

In the binary file, the address of the loop is: 40b7a2
